title: Capitalism and Freedom
author: Milton Friedman
subtitle: The Classic Argument for Economic Freedom and Individual Liberty
description: A landmark work of political and economic philosophy that argues competitive capitalism is a necessary condition for political freedom. Friedman makes the case for minimal government intervention, free markets, and individual choice as the foundation of a free society. First published in 1962, the book shaped the economic policies of Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher and remains one of the most influential libertarian texts ever written.
keyIdeas: The direct link between economic freedom and political freedom
Government's role should be limited to enforcing contracts, national defense, and addressing externalities
Negative income tax as a replacement for welfare bureaucracy
School vouchers to introduce competition in education
Voluntary exchange is the foundation of a free society
Occupational licensing as a barrier to economic opportunity
Stable monetary policy via a fixed money supply growth rule
The fallacy of 'free lunch' — all government programs have hidden costs
International free trade benefits all parties
Deregulation of professions and industries to lower costs
keyTakeaways: Economic freedom is a necessary condition for political freedom — you cannot have one without the other
Government intervention in markets almost always produces unintended negative consequences worse than the original problem
The 'public interest' justification for regulation is often a cover for protecting established interests from competition
A negative income tax would be more efficient and humane than the existing welfare system
School vouchers would improve education quality through competition and parental choice
Occupational licensing should be dramatically reduced to lower costs and increase opportunity
International free trade benefits all parties, even when trading partners have lower wages
The Federal Reserve should follow a fixed rule for money supply growth, not discretionary policy
Voluntary charity is superior to government-mandated redistribution
The proper role of government is limited: national defense, police, courts, and addressing genuine externalities
whoShouldRead: Anyone interested in the philosophical foundations of free-market capitalism
Students and scholars of economics, political science, and public policy
Libertarians and classical liberals seeking a rigorous articulation of their worldview
Policymakers and politicians from any ideological background
